missionBriefing: Welcome, Nature Guide! This 180-minute workshop, 'Nature Compass,' is designed for A2-level ESL students aged 10-12. Your mission is to lead them on an ornithological expedition through an autumn landscape. Using the 5E model, students will transform into scientists, observing bird adaptations, collecting data, and engineering solutions inspired by nature. Your role is to facilitate curiosity, ensure safety, and scaffold language to make complex scientific concepts accessible and exciting.

handbook.title: Nature Compass: Ornithology Explorer
handbook.visualPrompt: A vibrant cover illustration featuring a diverse group of children (ages 10-12) looking through binoculars at various birds in an autumn park setting. A large, stylized compass is in the foreground. Birds like a European Robin, a Jay, and a Ring-necked Parakeet are visible. The title is prominent and exciting. The overall style is a mix of cartoon and nature illustration, friendly and inviting.

handbook.title: Your Expedition Guide
handbook.visualPrompt: A clean, easy-to-read table of contents. Use small, simple icons next to each section title: a magnifying glass for 'Your Explorer Kit', a shield for 'Safety First', a bird icon for 'Birdwatching Data', etc. The layout should be spacious with a light, nature-themed background pattern (e.g., faint autumn leaves).

handbook.title: Safety First!
handbook.visualPrompt: A series of clear, simple icons in separate boxes, each representing a safety rule. For example, a 'stop' hand for 'Look, Don't Touch', a sun icon for 'Sun Protection', a group of people icon for 'Stay with Your Group', and a 'wash hands' icon. The text for each rule should be short and direct, placed directly below its icon.

handbook.title: Your Explorer Kit
handbook.visualPrompt: A checklist-style page. Each item (binoculars, notebook, pencil, magnifying glass, water bottle) is illustrated with a simple line drawing. Next to each illustration is the word for the item and a checkbox [ ] for students to tick. The layout resembles a packing list for an adventure.

handbook.title: Welcome, Nature Detectives!
handbook.visualPrompt: A half-page illustration of a wise old owl wearing a detective's hat, holding a magnifying glass. The owl is perched on a branch, looking directly at the reader with a friendly expression. The background is a soft-focus autumn forest.

handbook.title: What Do Birds Do?
handbook.visualPrompt: A mind-map style activity page. In the center is a circle with the question 'What do you know about birds?'. Several branches extend from the circle, leading to empty thought bubbles. A few small, simple bird illustrations are scattered around the page corners.

handbook.title: Birdwatching Secrets
handbook.visualPrompt: A comic-strip style layout with three panels. Panel 1 shows a child moving slowly and quietly. Panel 2 shows the child using binoculars correctly. Panel 3 shows the child listening carefully with a hand cupped to their ear. The style is instructional but fun.

handbook.title: My Birdwatching Data Sheet
handbook.visualPrompt: A structured data table with columns for 'Bird Name (or sketch)', 'Color', 'Size (Small/Medium/Large)', and 'Tally Marks (How many?)'. The headers have simple icons. The rows are clearly lined, providing ample space for students to write and draw. A small illustration of a pencil and a ruler is in the corner.

handbook.title: Amazing Adaptations!
handbook.visualPrompt: A central, large illustration of a generic bird. Arrows point from the illustration to different body parts (beak, feet, wings, feathers). Each arrow leads to a box containing a short explanation. The background is a simple, clean grid pattern to give it a scientific feel.

handbook.title: Vocabulary Match-Up
handbook.visualPrompt: A two-column matching activity. The left column has the vocabulary words (Beak, Plumage, Habitat, Migration) with a small icon next to each. The right column has simple definitions. Lines are meant to be drawn by the student to connect the word to its meaning. The layout is clean and uncluttered.

handbook.title: The Beak Challenge
handbook.visualPrompt: A blueprint-style page with a light blue background and white grid lines. A large, empty box in the center is labeled 'Design Your Bird Beak Here'. Around the box are smaller illustrations of different tools birds use their beaks for: cracking a nut, sipping nectar, catching a fish. This inspires the student's design.

handbook.title: My Beak Invention
handbook.visualPrompt: A two-part page. The top half has a space for a drawing, labeled 'My Beak Design'. The bottom half has lined spaces with prompts: 'My beak is for...', 'I used these materials...', and 'It is special because...'. This encourages students to explain their engineering choices.

handbook.title: My Top 3 Discoveries
handbook.visualPrompt: A visually appealing page designed like a scientist's journal. There are three numbered sections, each with a space for a small drawing and a few lines for writing. The background has a faint, textured paper look. A small compass rose icon is in the corner.

handbook.title: Certificate of Ornithology
handbook.visualPrompt: A formal-looking certificate with an ornate border made of leaves and feathers. There is a large space for the student's name, a line for the date, and a line for the instructor's signature. A gold seal graphic in the corner adds a touch of official recognition.

handbook.title: Nature Compass
handbook.visualPrompt: The back cover of the handbook. It features a large, beautifully illustrated compass with bird silhouettes marking North, South, East, and West. Below it, include the organization's logo and a small credit line. The background is a solid, calming color like forest green or sky blue.

phrases: It is perched on a branch.
What does its beak look like?
How does it survive in autumn?

permanent: Tablet or smartphone with a bird identification app (e.g., Merlin Bird ID)

safetyProtocol.risk: Contact with poisonous plants or allergens.

basicInfo.theme: Nature Compass: Ornithology and Autumn Adaptations

handbookStylePrompt: Create a visual style that is a blend of a classic nature field guide and a fun, modern activity book for kids. Use a warm, autumnal color palette (oranges, browns, deep greens, sky blues). Illustrations should be clear, scientifically accurate but with a friendly, slightly stylized feel. Typography should be clean, legible, and kid-friendly (e.g., a rounded sans-serif). Ensure plenty of white space to avoid overwhelming the reader. Icons and infographics should be simple and intuitive.
notebookLMPrompt: You are a 11-year-old ESL student and a nature detective. Your English is at an A2 level. You are excited to explore the park and find birds. Write about what you see and learn. Use simple sentences. You might draw pictures of the birds and label them. You can write about their colors, what they are doing, and what their beaks look like. For example: 'Today I go to park. I am a science detective! I see a small brown bird. It has a sharp beak. It eats seeds. I draw a picture of it. It is fun.'
visualReferences: Illustrations from 'The Lost Words' by Robert Macfarlane and Jackie Morris for the artistic, natural feel.
Layout style of National Geographic Kids activity books for engaging, clear design.
Iconography similar to the Merlin Bird ID app for simple, clear visual cues.
Color palette inspired by autumn photographs of Richmond Park.
